1.key_points: The patient should be in a 1/2 kneeling position with the front leg to be tested. Ensure the strap is perpendicular to the floor and properly tightened before zeroing your device. The dynamometer should be placed 2 fingers proximal to the base of the patella.
1.protocol_steps: • Position the patient in a 1/2 kneeling stance, with the front leg to be tested. The torso should be vertical, and the hip flexed to 90 degrees. • Place the ball of the patient’s foot on a solid block, ensuring proper alignment, keeping their shoes on. • Run the strap under the block and over the top of the knee, ensuring it is perpendicular to the floor. • Secure the dynamometer under the strap, positioning it 2 fingers proximal to the base of the patella. • Ensure the base of the 5th metatarsal is in line with the strap. • Tighten the strap to ensure the foot remains parallel to the floor when pushing. • Zero the dynamometer. • The patient may use a wall for balance during the test. • Instruct the patient to push as hard as possible for 3 seconds. • Rest for 20 seconds between repetitions. • Repeat the test for 3 repetitions and calculate the average score.
1.reference: Moraux, A., Canal, A., Ollivier, G., Ledoux, I., Doppler, V., Payan, C., & Hogrel, J. Y. (2013). Ankle dorsi-and plantar-flexion torques measured by dynamometry in healthy subjects from 5 to 80 years. BMC musculoskeletal disorders , 14 , 1-11.

2.equipment: Step or weight plate Metronome: We entered “Metronome” into Google search bar and set the one displayed to 60 beats per minute.
2.key_points: Ensure the patient keeps their test leg knee straight throughout the test. The patient is allowed to use two fingertips from each hand on a wall for balance. The test is performed at a frequency of 30 calf raises per minute, guided by a metronome set to 60 beats per minute. We have found that using a 15kg plate is more effective because, at a height of 3.8 cm, it allows the heel to lightly touch the floor during full dorsiflexion, providing a clear reference for the bottom position. Additionally, the plate offers better grip than a step, reducing the likelihood of the patient's foot slipping.
2.protocol_steps: • Provide verbal instructions to the patient and ask them to demonstrate to show understanding before beginning the test. • Have the patient stand on one leg with the knee straight and the forefoot supported on a step. • The non-tested leg should be bent to 90° of knee flexion, with the foot behind the body. • The patient is allowed to place two fingertips of each hand on the wall at shoulder height for balance. • Instruct the patient to raise their heel as high as possible while keeping the test leg’s knee straight, then return to the starting position for each repetition. • Emphasise that the patient must go “all the way back down” for full dorsiflexion range. • The patient should perform the test at a rate of 30 calf raises per minute, guided by a metronome set to 60 beats per minute. • Provide regular verbal encouragement throughout the test. • The test is stopped if the patient cannot maintain the pace, bends the test leg knee, moves forward instead of upward, applies more than their fingertips for balance, or stops due to fatigue. • Allow up to two attempts to correct form before stopping the test.
2.reference: Hebert-Losier, K., Fernandez, M. R., Athens, J., Kubo, M., & O’Neill, S. (2024). A randomised crossover trial on the effects of foot starting position on calf raise test outcomes: Position does matter. The Foot , 102112.

3.equipment: Phone inclinometer: We recommend Rate Fast Goniometer for iOS or Angle Meter for Android. Both are free to download.
3.key_points: Calibrate the inclinometer before testing. Place the inclinometer against the tibial tuberosity of the front leg with the long edge of the phone against the tibia. Ensure the patient keeps their heel in contact with the floor at all times.
3.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain and visually demonstrate the weight-bearing lunge test to the patient before testing. • Calibrate the inclinometer by placing it against a vertical surface (e.g. a wall) and ensuring it reads 0 degrees. • Have the patient stand an arm's distance away from the wall, facing it. • The patient flexes the hip and knee while dorsiflexing the ankle, lunging forward while keeping the sole of the foot fully in contact with the ground. • Once the patient reaches the maximal distance, they must maintain that position. • Measure the tibial inclination by placing the top of the smartphone against the tibial tuberosity. • Record three trials for each leg and calculate the mean of the three trials for each leg.
3.reference: Hankemeier, D. A., & Thrasher, A. B. (2014). Relationship between the weight-bearing lunge and nonweight-bearing dorsiflexion range of motion measures. Athletic Training & Sports Health Care , 6 (3), 128-134.

4.equipment: MyJump 2: Available to download on iOS and Android devices. Alternatives: Force plate or jump mat. Step (20cm in height).
4.key_points: Hands must remain on the hips throughout the entire movement. The patient should drop off the step rather than jump upwards off the step. The goal is to rebound off the floor as quickly as possible while jumping as high as possible.
4.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain and visually demonstrate the drop jump to the patient, emphasizing the importance of jumping as high as they can while getting off the floor as quickly as possible. • Instruct the patient to stand on a 20 cm step with feet shoulder-width apart, hands on hips, and knees fully extended. • Ask the patient to drop off the step, rather than jumping upwards, and immediately rebound off the floor. • The patient should jump as high as they can while keeping their knees fully extended and hands on their hips throughout the movement. • Keeping the knees fully extended in the air, rather than pulling the knees up towards the body.
4.reference: Wang, L. I., & Peng, H. T. (2014). Biomechanical comparisons of single-and double-legged drop jumps with changes in drop height. International journal of sports medicine , 35 (06), 522-527.

5.equipment: MyJump 2: Available to download on iOS and Android devices. Alternatives: Force plate or jump mat.
5.key_points: • Hands must remain on the hips throughout the entire movement. • The patient should drop off the step on the leg being tested e.g. if the left leg is being tested, drop off the left leg, rebound on the left leg, and land on the left leg. • The goal is to rebound off the floor as quickly as possible while jumping as high as possible on the same leg.
5.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain and visually demonstrate the single leg drop jump to the patient, emphasizing the importance of jumping as high as they can while getting off the floor as quickly as possible. • Instruct the patient to stand on a 20 cm step on one leg, with hands on hips and knees fully extended. • Ask the patient to drop off the step on one leg, rather than jumping upwards, and immediately rebound off the floor on the same leg. • The patient should jump as high as possible while keeping the knee fully extended and hands on hips throughout the movement. • After demonstrating, have the patient perform the movement to ensure understanding.
5.reference: Wang, L. I., & Peng, H. T. (2014). Biomechanical comparisons of single-and double-legged drop jumps with changes in drop height. International journal of sports medicine , 35 (06), 522-527.

6.key_points: The patient is positioned in supine, hands across by their sides. The shoulders must remain in contact with the bed throughout the test. The dynamometer pad or strap is centered on the forehead, just above the eyebrows. Perform three maximal efforts for 3 seconds each, with 20 seconds of rest between trials.
6.protocol_steps: • Position the patient in a supine position with their arms by their sides and hands resting on the bed. • Ensure the shoulders remain in contact with the bed. • Center the dynamometer pad or strap on the patient’s forehead, just superior to the eyebrows. • Adjust the bed so that the physiotherapist’s arms are straight during the test. • Instruct the patient to perform a submaximal effort as a practice repetition to familiarize them with the movement. • Ask the patient to push up against the dynamometer for 3 seconds, aiming to lift the back of their head off the bed without lifting their shoulders. • Perform three maximal effort trials with 20 seconds of rest between each trial. • Record the peak force for each trial and calculate the average of the three attempts.
6.reference: Krause, D. A., Hansen, K. A., Hastreiter, M. J., Kuhn, T. N., Peichel, M. L., & Hollman, J. H. (2019). A comparison of various cervical muscle strength testing methods using a handheld dynamometer. Sports health , 11 (1), 59-63.

7.key_points: The patient is positioned in supine with their hands by their sides The shoulders must remain in contact with the bed throughout the test. The dynamometer pad or strap is placed on the side of the forehead above the lateral corner of the eye. Perform three maximal efforts for 3 seconds each, with 20 seconds of rest between trials.
7.protocol_steps: • Position the patient in a supine position with their arms by their sides and hands resting on the bed. • Ensure the shoulders remain in contact with the bed. • Center the dynamometer pad or strap on the side of the forehead above the lateral corner of the eye. • Adjust the bed so that the physiotherapist’s arms are straight during the test. • Instruct the patient to perform a submaximal effort as a practice repetition to familiarize them with the movement. • Ask the patient to push against the dynamometer for 3 seconds, aiming to look to the side without rotating their torso. • Perform three maximal effort trials with 20 seconds of rest between each trial. • Record the peak force for each trial and calculate the average of the three attempts.

8.key_points: The patient is positioned in prone, arms by their sides. The patient is instructed to lift their head as if looking directly ahead, without lifting their chest off the bed. The dynamometer pad or strap is positioned slightly superior to the external occipital protuberance. Perform three maximal efforts for 3 seconds each, with 20 seconds of rest between trials.
8.protocol_steps: • Position the patient in a prone position with their arms by their sides, resting on the bed. • Instruct the patient to keep their chest on the bed and to lift their head as if they are looking straight ahead. • Place the dynamometer pad or strap slightly superior to the external occipital protuberance. • Adjust the bed so that the physiotherapist’s arms are straight during the test. • Ask the patient to perform a submaximal effort as a practice repetition to ensure they understand the movement. • Instruct the patient to push up against the dynamometer for 3 seconds, aiming to extend their neck without raising their chest. • Perform three maximal effort trials with 20 seconds of rest between each trial. • Record the peak force for each trial and calculate the average of the three attempts.
8.reference: Krause, D. A., Hansen, K. A., Hastreiter, M. J., Kuhn, T. N., Peichel, M. L., & Hollman, J. H. (2019). A comparison of various cervical muscle strength testing methods using a handheld dynamometer. Sports health , 11 (1), 59-63.

9.key_points: The patient is positioned in a side-lying position for lateral flexion. The dynamometer is placed on the lateral aspect of the head just superior to the ear. The patient is instructed to laterally flex their neck upward against the dynamometer. Perform three maximal efforts for 3 seconds each, with 20 seconds of rest between trials.
9.protocol_steps: • Position the patient in a side-lying position, ensuring their body is straight and their head is aligned with the spine by placing a pillow under their head. • Place the dynamometer pad or strap on the lateral aspect of the head just superior to the ear. • Adjust the bed and positioning to ensure that the physiotherapist’s arms remain straight during the test. • Instruct the patient to perform a submaximal effort as a practice repetition to familiarize them with the movement. • Ask the patient to push up against the dynamometer for 3 seconds, aiming to lift the side of their head off the bed without compensating with shoulder movement or torso rotation. • Perform three maximal effort trials with 20 seconds of rest between each trial. • Record the peak force for each trial and calculate the average of the three attempts.
9.reference: Krause, D. A., Hansen, K. A., Hastreiter, M. J., Kuhn, T. N., Peichel, M. L., & Hollman, J. H. (2019). A comparison of various cervical muscle strength testing methods using a handheld dynamometer. Sports health , 11 (1), 59-63.

10.key_points: The test is performed in a supine, hook-lying position (knees bent with feet flat on the table). The participant must maximally flex the chin and maintain a chin tuck position throughout the test, with their head 2.5cm off the bed. A line is drawn across the two approximating neck skin folds to monitor chin tuck maintenance. The examiner places a hand just below the participant's occiput to detect head contact.
10.protocol_steps: • Position the participant in a supine, hook-lying position with their knees bent and feet flat on the table. • Instruct the participant to maximally flex the chin and maintain the chin tuck throughout the test. • While maintaining the chin tuck, the participant lifts their head approximately 2.5 cm above the plinth. • Draw a line across the two approximating neck skin folds to monitor if the chin tuck is maintained. • The examiner places their hand below the occiput to detect any head contact. • Provide extra verbal commands if the edges of the line begin to separate or if the participant’s head touches the examiner’s hand. • The test is terminated if the lines no longer approximate due to loss of the chin tuck position or if the participant’s head remains in contact with the examiner’s hand for more than 1 second.
10.reference: Painkra, J. P., Kumar, S., Anwer, S., Kumar, R., Nezamuddin, M., & Equebal, A. (2014). Reliability of an assessment of deep neck flexor muscle endurance test: A cross-sectional study. International Journal of Therapy and Rehabilitation , 21 (5), 227-231.

11.equipment: Phone inclinometer: We recommend Rate Fast Goniometer for iOS or Angle Meter for Android. Both are free to download.
11.key_points: The test is performed with the patient seated, feet flat on the floor, back straight against the chair, eyes looking straight ahead, and arms at their sides. The smartphone is placed on the left side of the patient’s head, aligned with the external auditory meatus, to measure the range of motion. The patient is instructed to flex their neck (bring the chin toward the chest) without moving their back off the chair, or extreme effort.
11.protocol_steps: • Position the patient seated with their feet flat on the floor, back straight against the chair, and eyes looking forward. • Place the smartphone on the left side of the patient’s head, aligned with the external auditory meatus. • Instruct the patient to flex their neck, bringing their chin toward their chest while maintaining contact between their back and the chair, without excessive force. • Ensure the researcher’s hand remains in contact with both the smartphone and the patient’s head to ensure the phone moves with the patient. • Perform three attempts of cervical flexion, and record the measurement for each attempt, before calculating the mean.
11.reference: Monreal, C., Luinstra, L., Larkins, L., & May, J. (2020). Validity and intrarater reliability using a smartphone clinometer application to measure active cervical range of motion including rotation measurements in supine. Journal of Sport Rehabilitation , 30 (4), 680-684.

12.equipment: Phone inclinometer: We recommend Rate Fast Goniometer for iOS or Angle Meter for Android. Both are free to download.
12.key_points: The test is performed with the patient lying supine on a treatment bed. The smartphone is placed on the patient’s head with the display arrow aligned with the nose and the display interface bar parallel to an imaginary line between the acromial processes. The patient is instructed to rotate their head at their own pace without compensation or extreme effort.
12.protocol_steps: • Position the patient in a supine position on a treatment bed. • Place the smartphone on the patient’s head, ensuring the display arrow is aligned with the nose and the display interface bar is parallel to an imaginary line between the acromial processes. • Instruct the patient to rotate their head at their own pace, while keeping both shoulders on the bed to prevent them from compensating through their torso. • The patient should move within a comfortable range, without extreme effort. • Ensure the researcher’s hands remain in contact with both the smartphone and the patient’s head to ensure the phone moves with the patient. • Perform three attempts of cervical rotation in each direction (left and right), and record the average measurement of each attempt.
12.reference: Monreal, C., Luinstra, L., Larkins, L., & May, J. (2020). Validity and intrarater reliability using a smartphone clinometer application to measure active cervical range of motion including rotation measurements in supine. Journal of Sport Rehabilitation , 30 (4), 680-684.

13.equipment: Phone inclinometer: We recommend Rate Fast Goniometer for iOS or Angle Meter for Android. Both are free to download.
13.key_points: The test is performed with the patient seated, feet flat on the floor, back straight against the chair, eyes looking straight ahead, and arms at their sides. The smartphone is placed on the left side of the patient’s head, aligned with the external auditory meatus, to measure the range of motion. The patient is instructed to extend their neck (look upward) without compensation or extreme effort.
13.protocol_steps: • Position the patient seated with their feet flat on the floor, back straight against the chair, and eyes looking forward. • Place the smartphone on the left side of the patient’s head, aligned with the external auditory meatus. • Instruct the patient to extend their neck, looking upward as far as comfortable without compensation or excessive force. • Ensure the researcher’s hand remains in contact with both the smartphone and the patient’s head to ensure the phone moves with the patient. • Perform three attempts of cervical extension, and record the average of the three measurements.
13.reference: Monreal, C., Luinstra, L., Larkins, L., & May, J. (2020). Validity and intrarater reliability using a smartphone clinometer application to measure active cervical range of motion including rotation measurements in supine. Journal of Sport Rehabilitation , 30 (4), 680-684.

14.equipment: Phone inclinometer: We recommend Rate Fast Goniometer for iOS or Angle Meter for Android. Both are free to download.
14.key_points: The test is performed with the patient seated, feet flat on the floor, back straight against the chair, eyes looking straight ahead, and arms at their sides. The smartphone is placed on the contralateral side of the patient’s head, with the display aligned with the eyes, to measure the range of motion. The patient is instructed to laterally flex their neck (ear toward shoulder) while keeping their torso still.
14.protocol_steps: • Position the patient seated with their feet flat on the floor, back straight against the chair, and eyes looking forward. • Place the smartphone on the contralateral side of the patient’s head, with the display level aligned with the patient’s eyes. • Instruct the patient to laterally flex their neck, bringing their ear toward their shoulder without leaning over through their torso or excessive force. • Ensure the researcher’s hand remains in contact with both the smartphone and the patient’s head to ensure the phone moves with the patient. • Perform three attempts of cervical lateral flexion, and record the average measurement of the three trials.
14.reference: Monreal, C., Luinstra, L., Larkins, L., & May, J. (2020). Validity and intrarater reliability using a smartphone clinometer application to measure active cervical range of motion including rotation measurements in supine. Journal of Sport Rehabilitation , 30 (4), 680-684.

15.key_points: The patient is positioned supine with the shoulder in a neutral position and the elbow flexed to 90°. The upper arm is kept against the trunk to avoid compensatory movements. The non testing arm should be holding the table to prevent the patient from moving.
15.protocol_steps: • Position the patient supine on a treatment table with the shoulder neutral and elbow flexed to 90°, ensuring that the upper arm remains stabilized against the body. • Place the dynamometer just proximal to the styloid process of the radius. • The testing arm should be fully pronated (palm facing upwards). • Instruct the patient to flex their elbow, performing maximal elbow flexion into the dynamometer for 3 seconds. • Allow the patient to rest for 20 seconds. • Repeat the test three times and record the average of the three maximal efforts.
15.reference: Douma, R. K., Soer, R., Krijnen, W. P., Reneman, M., & Van Der Schans, C. P. (2014). Reference values for isometric muscle force among workers for the Netherlands: a comparison of reference values. BMC sports science, medicine and rehabilitation , 6 , 1-10.

16.key_points: The patient is positioned supine with the shoulder in a neutral position and the elbow flexed to 90°. The upper arm is kept against the trunk to avoid compensatory movements. The other arm is used to hold the bed to prevent the patient moving.
16.protocol_steps: • Position the patient supine on a treatment table with the shoulder neutral and elbow flexed to 90°, ensuring that the upper arm remains stabilized against the body. • Their testing arm should be fully pronated (palm facing upwards). • Place the dynamometer just proximal to the styloid process of the radius. • Instruct the patient to extend their elbow, performing maximal elbow extension into the dynamometer for 3 seconds. • Allow the patient to rest for 20 seconds. • Repeat the test three times and record the average of the three maximal efforts.
16.reference: Douma, R. K., Soer, R., Krijnen, W. P., Reneman, M., & Van Der Schans, C. P. (2014). Reference values for isometric muscle force among workers for the Netherlands: a comparison of reference values. BMC sports science, medicine and rehabilitation , 6 , 1-10.

17.equipment: Phone inclinometer: We recommend Rate Fast Goniometer for iOS or Angle Meter for Android. Both are free to download.
17.key_points: The test is performed with the patient standing upright, with the elbows tucked by the sides to minimise shoulder movement. A pen is gripped in the hand to maintain consistent alignment and the patient is told to avoid flexion or extension of the wrist. The smartphone is zeroed at the vertical axis before each test to ensure accurate measurement.
17.protocol_steps: • Position the patient standing. • Ensure the elbows are tucked by the sides to prevent shoulder involvement. • Ask the patient to grip a pen for alignment so the pen is running along a vertical axis. • Zero the smartphone or measurement device at the vertical axis to ensure accuracy. • Place the long axis of the measurement device against the dorsal aspect of the patients forearm, at the level of the joint line of the wrist. • Instruct the patient to perform a submaximal effort for pronation to familiarise them with the movement. • Ask the patient to turn their palm downwards (pronate) as much as possible while maintaining elbow contact with the torso. • Record the angle of pronation. • Perform three trials and calculate the average angle.
17.reference: Koong, D. P., Lee, J., Cheng, T. L., & Little, D. G. (2020). Validity and reliability of smartphone inclinometer applications for measurement of elbow range of motion in paediatric patients. Journal of Children's Orthopaedics , 14 (5), 488-494.

18.equipment: Phone inclinometer: We recommend Rate Fast Goniometer for iOS or Angle Meter for Android. Both are free to download.
18.key_points: The test is performed with the patient standing upright, with the elbows tucked by the sides to minimise shoulder movement. A pen is gripped in the hand to maintain consistent alignment and the patient is told to avoid flexion or extension of the wrist. The smartphone is zeroed at the vertical axis before each test to ensure accurate measurement.
18.protocol_steps: • Position the patient standing. • Ensure the elbows are tucked by the sides to prevent shoulder involvement. • Ask the patient to grip a pen for alignment so the pen is running along a vertical axis. • Zero the smartphone or measurement device at the vertical axis to ensure accuracy. • Place the long axis of the measurement device against the palmar aspect of the patients forearm, at the level of the joint line of the wrist. • Instruct the patient to perform a submaximal effort for supination to familiarise them with the movement. • Ask the patient to turn their palm upwards (supinate) as much as possible while maintaining elbow contact with the torso. • Record the angle of supination. • Perform three trials and calculate the average angle.
18.reference: Koong, D. P., Lee, J., Cheng, T. L., & Little, D. G. (2020). Validity and reliability of smartphone inclinometer applications for measurement of elbow range of motion in paediatric patients. Journal of Children's Orthopaedics , 14 (5), 488-494.

19.key_points: The test can be performed in two variations: the standard position on hands and toes for men, or the modified position on hands and knees for women. The subject must maintain a straight and rigid body position throughout the test, with hands placed shoulder-width apart. The goal is to complete as many push-ups as possible within the allotted time, maintaining proper form.
19.protocol_steps: • Position the subject prone with hands shoulder-width apart. For men, use the hands and toes position; for women, use the hands and knees position. • Instruct the subject to maintain a straight body posture from the shoulders to the feet (or knees in the modified version), limiting trunk and head motion during the movement. • Begin the test with the subject in the up position, elbows fully extended. • Direct the subject to lower their body by flexing the elbows until the upper arm is parallel to the testing surface, then return to the starting position as quickly as possible. • Perform as many repetitions as possible in 15 seconds. • Record the number of push-ups completed during the trial. • Verbal encouragement should be provided throughout to maintain high-intensity effort.
19.reference: Negrete, R. J., Hanney, W. J., Kolber, M. J., Davies, G. J., Ansley, M. K., McBride, A. B., & Overstreet, A. L. (2010). Reliability, minimal detectable change, and normative values for tests of upper extremity function and power. The Journal of Strength & Conditioning Research , 24 (12), 3318-3325.

20.key_points: The patient should be positioned in side-lying with the top leg bent and resting on the bed. Ensure the bottom leg is straight, with the foot hanging off the end of the bed. The dynamometer should be placed 2 cm proximal to the medial malleolus of the bottom leg.
20.protocol_steps: • Position the patient in a side-lying position, with the top leg’s hip and knee bent to 90 degrees, and the foot resting on the bed. • The bottom leg should remain straight, with the foot hanging off the edge of the bed. • Ensure the patient’s shoulders and hips are stacked vertically on top of each other. • Lower the bed to allow the physiotherapist to keep their elbows fully extended while performing the test. • Secure the dynamometer 2 cm proximal to the medial malleolus of the bottom leg. • Instruct the patient to lift their bottom leg off the bed, pressing into the dynamometer with maximal effort for 3 seconds. • Repeat the test three times, and calculate the average score.
20.reference: Thorborg, K., Petersen, J., Magnusson, S. P., & Hölmich, P. (2010). Clinical assessment of hip strength using a hand‐held dynamometer is reliable. Scandinavian journal of medicine & science in sports , 20 (3), 493-501.

21.key_points: The patient should be positioned in side-lying with legs stacked on top of each other. Ensure both legs are straight, with the dynamometer placed 2 cm proximal to the lateral malleolus of the top leg.
21.protocol_steps: • Position the patient in a side-lying position with both legs stacked on top of each other, keeping both legs straight. • Ensure the patient’s shoulders and hips are aligned and stacked vertically. • Lower the bed to allow the physiotherapist to maintain fully extended elbows during the test. • Secure the dynamometer 2 cm proximal to the lateral malleolus of the top leg. • Instruct the patient to lift their top leg off the bed, pressing into the dynamometer with maximal effort for 3 seconds. • Repeat the test three times, and calculate the average score.
21.reference: Thorborg, K., Petersen, J., Magnusson, S. P., & Hölmich, P. (2010). Clinical assessment of hip strength using a hand‐held dynamometer is reliable. Scandinavian journal of medicine & science in sports , 20 (3), 493-501.

22.key_points: Calibrate the dynamometer with nothing touching it before beginning the test. The dynamometer is placed two fingers' width proximal to the base of the patella. The physiotherapist resists the patient's push, rather than pushing back against them. Three measures are taken, and the average is recorded.
22.protocol_steps: • Calibrate the dynamometer to ensure an accurate baseline with no external forces acting on it. • Instruct the patient to lie supine on the treatment table, with one leg fully extended and the test leg flexed to 90° at the hip and knee. • Ask the patient to hold onto the sides of the bed to prevent slipping during the test. • The physiotherapist should stand at the head of the bed with their elbows fully extended. • Place the dynamometer two fingers' width proximal to the base of the patient's patella on the test leg. • Ask the patient to push against the dynamometer as hard as they can while you resist their push, ensuring you maintain steady resistance without pushing back into them. • Take three separate measurements before calculating and recording the average of the three measurements.
22.reference: Thorborg, K., Petersen, J., Magnusson, S. P., & Hölmich, P. (2010). Clinical assessment of hip strength using a hand‐held dynamometer is reliable. Scandinavian journal of medicine & science in sports, 20(3), 493-501.

23.key_points: Calibrate the dynamometer with nothing touching it before the test. The bed is set low so the physiotherapist can stand with their elbows fully extended. The patient extends from the hip rather than flexing from the knee. The physiotherapist resists the patient’s push, rather than pushing back against them.
23.protocol_steps: • Calibrate the dynamometer to ensure an accurate baseline with no external forces acting on it. • Instruct the patient to lie in a prone position on the treatment table, with both legs fully extended. • Adjust the height of the bed to a low position so that you, can stand comfortably with your elbows fully extended during the test. • Place the dynamometer 2 cm proximal to the lateral malleolus, just above the Achilles tendon on the test leg. • Ask the patient to lift their foot off the table without bending their knee, so that they extend from their hip. • They should push as hard as they can against the dynamometer while you resist their movement, ensuring you maintain steady resistance without pushing back. • Take three separate measurements before calculating and recording the average of the three measurements.
23.reference: Thorborg, K., Petersen, J., Magnusson, S. P., & Hölmich, P. (2010). Clinical assessment of hip strength using a hand‐held dynamometer is reliable. Scandinavian journal of medicine & science in sports, 20(3), 493-501.

24.key_points: • The patient should sit with hips and knees flexed to 90 degrees, feet off the floor, and a rolled-up towel between their knees. • The dynamometer is placed 2 cm proximal to the lateral malleolus of the testing leg. • Ensure the physiotherapist is in a half-kneeling position to secure the dynamometer.
24.protocol_steps: • Position the patient seated over the side of the bed, with hips and knees flexed to 90 degrees, and feet off the floor. • Place a rolled-up towel between the patient’s knees, keeping it in place by squeezing their knees together • The patient should cross their arms over their chest and sit tall. • Secure the dynamometer 2 cm proximal to the lateral malleolus of the testing leg. • The physiotherapist should be in a half-kneeling position, with the elbow of the arm holding the dynamometer tucked onto their anterior superior iliac spine for stability. • Instruct the patient to push their ankle outward, away from the other leg, focusing on internal rotation. • Ask the patient to push for 3 seconds with maximal effort. • Repeat the test three times, and calculate the average score.
24.reference: Thorborg, K., Petersen, J., Magnusson, S. P., & Hölmich, P. (2010). Clinical assessment of hip strength using a hand‐held dynamometer is reliable. Scandinavian journal of medicine & science in sports , 20 (3), 493-501.

25.key_points: The patient should sit over the side of the bed, with hips and knees flexed to 90 degrees, feet off the floor, and a rolled-up towel between their knees. The dynamometer is placed 2 cm proximal to the medial malleolus of the testing leg. Ensure the physiotherapist is in a half-kneeling position to secure the dynamometer.
25.protocol_steps: • Position the patient seated over the side of the bed, with hips and knees flexed to 90 degrees, and feet off the floor. • Place a rolled-up towel between the patient’s knees, and ask them to tuck the non-testing leg back so it’s out of the way. • The patient should cross their arms over their chest and sit tall. • Secure the dynamometer 2 cm proximal to the medial malleolus of the testing leg. • The physiotherapist should be in a half-kneeling position, with the elbow of the arm holding the dynamometer tucked onto their anterior superior iliac spine for stability. • Instruct the patient to rotate their ankle inwards towards the dynamometer, focusing on external rotation rather than hip adduction. • Ask the patient to push for 3 seconds with maximal effort. • Repeat the test three times, and calculate the average score.
25.reference: Thorborg, K., Petersen, J., Magnusson, S. P., & Hölmich, P. (2010). Clinical assessment of hip strength using a hand‐held dynamometer is reliable. Scandinavian journal of medicine & science in sports , 20 (3), 493-501.

26.equipment: Phone inclinometer: We recommend Rate Fast Goniometer for iOS or Angle Meter for Android. Both are free to download.
26.key_points: Ensure the lumbar spine remains in contact with the table and that the thigh does not abduct during the test. The hip angle is recorded based on the position of the thigh relative to the horizontal plane.
26.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain and visually demonstrate the modified Thomas test to the patient, emphasising proper body positioning and the importance of keeping the lumbar spine in contact with the table. • Instruct the patient to sit as close to the edge of the examination table as possible. • Ask the patient to support their non testing thigh while you assist them in slowly rolling backward onto the table. • Flex the non-testing hip until the patient’s lumbar lordosis is flattened against the table and allow the test leg to hang unsupported off the table. • Palpate the patient’s lumbar spine to confirm it remains in contact with the table during the test. • Monitor the test leg to ensure that the thigh does not abduct. • Position the long axis of the phone along the iliotibial band, half way between the greater trochanter and lateral epicondyle. • Record the hip angle: • If the thigh is at the horizontal plane, record the hip angle as 0°. • If the thigh is above the horizontal, record the hip angle as a negative number. • If the thigh is below the horizontal, record the hip angle as a positive number. • Repeat the procedure on the opposite side.
26.reference: Clapis, P. A., Davis, S. M., & Davis, R. O. (2008). Reliability of inclinometer and goniometric measurements of hip extension flexibility using the modified Thomas test. Physiotherapy theory and practice, 24(2), 135-141.

27.equipment: Phone inclinometer: We recommend Rate Fast Goniometer for iOS or Angle Meter for Android. Both are free to download.
27.key_points: Calibrate the inclinometer to ensure it reads 0 when placed against a vertical surface. A yoga block or rolled-up towel should be placed between the knees, which should be gently squeezed together. The measurement is taken with the long axis of the phone placed midway up the medial border of the tibia.
27.protocol_steps: • Calibrate the inclinometer by placing it against a vertical surface, such as a wall, to ensure it reads 0. • Instruct the patient to sit on the edge of the treatment table with their legs hanging off the edge, and their hips and knees flexed to 90 degrees. • Place a yoga block or rolled-up towel between the patient’s knees and ask them to hold it in place by gently squeezing their knees together. • Ask the patient to actively internally rotate their hips to their end range simultaneously. • Position the long axis of the phone against the medial border of the tibia, approximately halfway up. • Measure and record the range of hip internal rotation.
27.reference: Krause, D. A., Hollman, J. H., Krych, A. J., Kalisvaart, M. M., & Levy, B. A. (2015). Reliability of hip internal rotation range of motion measurement using a digital inclinometer. Knee Surgery, Sports Traumatology, Arthroscopy, 23(9), 2562-2567.

28.equipment: Chair or plyo box (60cm in height).
28.key_points: The box is 60 cm high, with the test leg should be in approximately 20° knee flexion and the arms crossed over the chest throughout the test. The non-working leg must remain stationary in a vertical position. The patient’s hips must touch the ground between repetitions without resting, and the hip must extend to 0°.
28.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain and visually demonstrate the heel box lift to the patient, and ask them to demonstrate a few practice repetitions to ensure understanding. • Instruct the patient to lie down on the ground with one heel on a 60 cm high box and the test leg in approximately 20° knee flexion. • Ask the patient to cross their arms over their chest and push down through the heel to lift their hips off the ground. • The goal is to perform as many repetitions as possible until failure, with consistent feedback provided to ensure correct technique. • Ensure that the non-working leg remains stationary in a vertical position to prevent momentum from aiding the movement. • Each repetition should include the patient touching their hips to the ground without resting, followed by extending the hip to 0°. • If the patient loses correct form, provide one warning and cease the test at the next fault in technique. • Record the maximum number of repetitions achieved. • Repeat the test on the opposite leg following the same procedure.
28.reference: Freckleton, G., Cook, J., & Pizzari, T. (2014). The predictive validity of a single leg bridge test for hamstring injuries in Australian Rules Football Players. British journal of sports medicine , 48 (8), 713-717.

29.key_points: Hands and free leg are allowed to swing naturally during the hop. The patient must hold their landing on one leg for 3 seconds. The distance is measured from the toe of the push-off foot to the heel of the landing foot.
29.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain and visually demonstrate the single leg hop to the subject, emphasising the importance of a controlled and balanced landing. Ask the patient to demonstrate in a practice trial. • Instruct the patient to stand on the test leg with their toes aligned with 0 on the tape measure. • Ask the patient to hop as far as possible and land on the same leg allowing the arms and non-testing leg to swing naturally. • The patient should perform a controlled, balanced landing and keep the landing foot in place. • Measure the distance from the first toe at push-off to the heel at the landing position in centimetres. • Ensure that no extra hops occur after landing before measuring.
29.reference: Almalki, H., Herrington, L., & Jones, R. (2021). Normative values for single-leg hop performance in Saudi healthy population. Saudi Journal of Sports Medicine , 21 (3), 87-92.

30.key_points: • Ensure the dynamometer is externally fixed, with the strap 2cm above the malleolus. • Ask the patient to hold the treatment table with their arms extended behind them. • Make sure the test is comfortable, consider placing a towel under the patients knee for comfort.
30.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test to the patient before testing and ask them to perform a submaximal practice repetition to ensure they understand. • Externally fix the dynamometer to the treatment table with a strap or traction belt. Loop the other end of the strap 2 cm above the malleolus with the patient’s leg hanging off the table at a 90° knee flexion. • Zero the dynamometer, ensuring the patient is not exerting any force during this step. • Instruct the patient to sit upright, with knees at 90° flexion, hands holding the back of the treatment table for support, chest up, and looking forward. • Instruct the patient to stabilize by remaining still in the starting position for 2-3 seconds before each repetition. • The patient extends the knee of the leg connected to the dynamometer with maximal force outward. • Hold the knee extension for 3 seconds, then return to the starting position with the knee flexed at 90°. Rest for 20 seconds • Repeat the test for 3 repetitions on each leg and use the average result for each side.
30.reference: Koblbauer, I. F., Lambrecht, Y., Van Der Hulst, M. L., Neeter, C., Engelbert, R. H., Poolman, R. W., & Scholtes, V. A. (2011). Reliability of maximal isometric knee strength testing with modified hand-held dynamometry in patients awaiting total knee arthroplasty: useful in research and individual patient settings? A reliability study. BMC musculoskeletal disorders , 12 , 1-9.

31.equipment: Handheld dynamometer. Flat surface such as a chair or plyo box (40cm in height).
31.key_points: Remove shoes prior to testing. The non tested leg should be in full extension. Ask the patient to keep their hips on the floor rather than lifting into a bridge.
31.protocol_steps: • Position the patient supine, with knees and hips flexed to 90°, and remove their shoes. • Place the dynamometer on a flat surface 16 inches (40 cm) off the floor and calibrate it with nothing touching it. • The patient should place the heel of the limb to be tested directly on top of the dynamometer. • The other limb should be fully extended and resting on the floor next to the flat surface. • Instruct the patient to relax the testing limb as much as possible. • Ask the patient to complete three maximal isometric contractions, each lasting 3 seconds, with a rest interval of 20 seconds between efforts.
31.reference: McCall, A., Nedelec, M., Carling, C., Le Gall, F., Berthoin, S., & Dupont, G. (2015). Reliability and sensitivity of a simple isometric posterior lower limb muscle test in professional football players. Journal of sports sciences, 33(12), 1298-1304

32.key_points: Demonstrate the desired movement before testing. Ensure the patient maintains their non-testing leg in a comfortable position without touching the ground. The patient must perform as many repetitions as possible in 30 seconds.
32.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain and visually demonstrate the single leg sit-to-stand movement to the patient before testing. • Allow the patient to complete two practice repetitions to confirm their understanding of the movement. • The patient starts seated on an 18-inch (45.7 cm) chair with the testing leg's knee flexed to 90 degrees. • Instruct the patient to rise to full knee extension on the testing leg, ensuring the non-testing leg does not touch the ground. • The patient may keep their non-testing leg in a self-selected comfortable position, ranging from fully extended to nearly 90 degrees of flexion. • After standing, the patient descends until their buttocks touch the chair. The patient may briefly touch the chair without bearing weight through their buttocks before moving on to the next repetition. • The patient performs the test with their arms crossed over their chest. • If the movement is performed incorrectly (e.g., buttocks do not touch the chair, full knee extension is not reached, or the non-testing leg touches the ground), the repetition is not counted. • Instruct the patient to perform as many correct repetitions as possible within 30 seconds.
32.reference: Waldhelm, A., Gubler, C., Sullivan, K., Witte, C., Buchheister, D., & Bartz-Broussard, J. (2020). Inter-rater and test-retest reliability of two new single leg sit-to-stand tests. International Journal of Sports Physical Therapy , 15 (3), 388.

33.equipment: Phone inclinometer: We recommend Rate Fast Goniometer for iOS or Angle Meter for android. Both are free to download.
33.key_points: Calibrate the inclinometer before testing. Place the inclinometer against the tibial tuberosity of the front leg with the long edge of the phone against the tibia. Ensure the patient keeps their thigh in contact with the bed at all times.
33.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain and visually demonstrate the test to the patient before testing. • Calibrate the inclinometer by placing it against a horizontal surface (e.g. the bed) and ensuring it reads 0 degrees. • Place the patient in a supine position on the bed. • The patient extends their knee, lifting their heel off the bed while keeping the back of their knee in contact with the bed • Measure the tibial inclination by placing the top of the smartphone against the tibial tuberosity. • Record three trials for each leg and calculate the mean of the three trials for each leg.
33.reference: Peters, P. G., Herbenick, M. A., Anloague, P. A., Markert, R. J., & Rubino, L. J. (2011). Knee range of motion: reliability and agreement of 3 measurement methods. The American Journal of Orthopedics, 40(12).

34.equipment: Phone inclinometer: We recommend Rate Fast Goniometer for iOS or Angle Meter for android. Both are free to download.
34.key_points: Calibrate the inclinometer before testing. Place the inclinometer against the tibial tuberosity of the front leg with the long edge of the phone against the tibia. Ensure the patient keeps their thigh in contact with the bed at all times.
34.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain and visually demonstrate the test to the patient before testing. • Calibrate the inclinometer by placing it against a horizontal surface (e.g. the bed) and ensuring it reads 0 degrees. • Place the patient in a prone position on the bed with their feet hanging over the edge so their tibia is in contact with the bed • The patient flexes their knee, pulling their heel towards them while keeping their thigh in contact with the bed • Measure the tibial inclination by placing the top of the smartphone against the tibial tuberosity. • Record three trials for each leg and calculate the mean of the three trials for each leg.
34.reference: Mohammad, W. S., Elattar, F. F., Elsais, W. M., & AlDajah, S. O. (2021). Validity and Reliability of a Smartphone and Digital Inclinometer in Measuring the Lower Extremity Joints Range of Motion. Montenegrin Journal of Sports Science & Medicine , 10 (2).

35.equipment: MyJump 2: Available to download on iOS and Android devices. Alternatives: Force plate or jump mat.
35.key_points: Hands must remain on the waist throughout the jump. The patient must perform a quick downward movement followed by a fast upward movement to jump as high as possible. The patient must keep their knees fully extended in the air rather than bringing them up to their chest.
35.protocol_steps: • Instruct the patient to stand with a straight torso and knees fully extended, feet shoulder-width apart. • Ask the patient to keep their hands on their waist throughout the jump. • The patient should perform a quick downward movement, achieving approximately 90° of knee flexion. • Follow this with a fast upward movement to jump as high as possible keeping the knees fully extended.
35.reference: Chow, G. C. C., Kong, Y. H., & Pun, W. Y. (2023). The concurrent validity and test-retest reliability of possible remote assessments for measuring countermovement jump: My jump 2, HomeCourt & Takei vertical jump meter. Applied Sciences , 13 (4), 2142.

36.equipment: MyJump 2: Available to download on iOS and Android devices. Alternatives: Force plate or jump mat.
36.key_points: Hands must remain on the waist throughout the jump. The patient must be balanced on one leg before starting the movement The patient must keep their testing leg fully extended in the air rather than bringing it up to their chest.
36.protocol_steps: • Instruct the patient to stand on one leg with one leg bent so the hip and knee are at approximately 45 degrees. • Ask the patient to keep their hands on their waist throughout the jump. • The patient should perform a quick downward movement, achieving approximately 90° of knee flexion. • Follow this with a fast upward movement to jump as high as possible keeping the testing leg fully extended. • The non testing leg must not be used to generate momentum by swinging excessively.
36.reference: Chow, G. C. C., Kong, Y. H., & Pun, W. Y. (2023). The concurrent validity and test-retest reliability of possible remote assessments for measuring countermovement jump: My jump 2, HomeCourt & Takei vertical jump meter. Applied Sciences , 13 (4), 2142.

37.key_points: The test is performed in the “Biering-Sorensen position” with the upper body extending out over the end of a test bench. The edge of the bed is aligned with the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) of the patient. During the test, the patients ankles are secured with a traction belt. Additionally, a second belt is placed around the torso, positioned just below the arms, and is connected to the dynamometer. The dynamometer is then anchored to the base of the bed or an immovable object (we used two 25kg dumb bells in the video). The arms are crossed over the chest with hands resting on the opposite shoulders.
37.protocol_steps: • Position the patient in a prone position with the ASIS of their hips on the edge of the treatment table. • Connect the dynamometer to the base of the table place the belt round the torso of the patient. • Instruct the patient to pull up maximally against the dynamometer, aiming to extend the spine. Perform three 5-second maximum efforts with 20 seconds between trials. • Allow the patient to rest by placing their arms on a chair between repetitions. • Start each attempt with a countdown “3, 2, 1, pull.” The dynamometer should be zeroed between each trial. Record peak force values in Newtons, and calculate the mean of the three trials.
37.reference: Bucke, J., Mattiussi, A., May, K., & Shaw, J. (2024). The reliability, variability and minimal detectable change of multiplanar isometric trunk strength testing using a fixed digital dynamometer. Journal of Sports Sciences , 1-7.

38.key_points: The test is performed with the participant in a supine position on the treatment table, aligned so that the edge of the bed is at the posterior superior iliac spine (PSIS). The participant’s ankles are secured with a traction belt, and an additional belt is placed around the torso, positioned just below the arms, and connected to the dynamometer. The dynamometer is anchored to the base of the bed or an immovable object (we used two 25kg dumb bells in the video). The arms are crossed over the chest with hands resting on the opposite shoulders.
38.protocol_steps: • Position the participant in a supine position with their PSIS aligned with the edge of the treatment table. • Attach the dynamometer to the base of the table and place the belt around the participant’s torso, just below the axilla. • Instruct the participant to pull up maximally against the dynamometer, aiming to flex the spine. Perform three 5-second maximal efforts with a 20-second rest between trials. • Allow the participant to rest by lying back or placing their arms on a chair/box between repetitions. • Begin each attempt with a countdown: “3, 2, 1, pull.” Ensure the dynamometer is zeroed between each trial. Record peak force values in Newtons and calculate the mean of the three trials.
38.reference: Bucke, J., Mattiussi, A., May, K., & Shaw, J. (2024). The reliability, variability and minimal detectable change of multiplanar isometric trunk strength testing using a fixed digital dynamometer. Journal of Sports Sciences , 1-7.

39.key_points: The test is performed with the participant lying on their side on the treatment table, so that their iliac crest is aligned with the edge of the table. The top leg is positioned in front of the bottom leg for support. The participant's ankles are secured with a traction belt, and an additional belt is placed around the torso, positioned just below the arms, and connected to the dynamometer. The arms are crossed over the chest with hands resting on the opposite shoulders.
39.protocol_steps: • Position the participant in a side-lying position on the treatment table, so that their iliac crest is aligned with the edge of the table. • The top leg should be placed in front of the bottom leg for support. • Attach the dynamometer to the base of the table or an immovable object (we used two 25kg dumb bells in the video). and place the belt around the participant’s torso. • Instruct the participant to pull up maximally against the dynamometer, aiming to side-flex the spine. Perform three 5-second maximal efforts with a 20-second rest between trials. • Allow the participant to rest by placing their arms on a chair between repetitions. • Begin each attempt with a countdown: “3, 2, 1, pull.” Ensure the dynamometer is zeroed between each trial. Record peak force values in Newtons and calculate the mean of the three trials.
39.reference: Bucke, J., Mattiussi, A., May, K., & Shaw, J. (2024). The reliability, variability and minimal detectable change of multiplanar isometric trunk strength testing using a fixed digital dynamometer. Journal of Sports Sciences , 1-7.

40.key_points: The test is performed with the participant in a tall kneeling position on the floor, with their knees shoulder-width apart. The arms are fully extended at shoulder height, holding the traction belt in both hands. The dynamometer is secured to a squat rack with the traction belt aligned parallel to the participant's shoulders. The objective is for the participant to rotate their torso maximally, pulling the belt away from the rack.
40.protocol_steps: • Position the participant in a tall kneeling stance with knees shoulder-width apart and arms fully extended at shoulder height, holding the traction belt in both hands. • Attach the dynamometer to a squat rack using the traction belt, ensuring the belt is parallel to the participant's shoulders. • Instruct the participant to rotate their torso as hard as possible to pull the belt away from the rack. Perform three 3-second maximal efforts with a 20-second rest between trials. • Allow the participant to rest between repetitions. • Begin each attempt with a countdown: “3, 2, 1, pull.” Ensure the dynamometer is zeroed between each trial. Record peak force values in Newtons and calculate the mean of the three trials.

41.equipment: Medicine ball (2.7kg in weight). Tape measure.
41.key_points: Position the patient so that they are seated on the end of a standard adjustable weightlifting bench in a flat position. The feet remain flat on the floor throughout the movement. The starting position involves the participant forward-flexing at the hips and abdomen while rotating to either the left or right side. The forward flexion and rotation enhance the power of the movement as the participant throws a 2.7-kg medicine ball to the contralateral side.
41.protocol_steps: • Position the participant seated at the end of the weightlifting bench in a flat position, with their feet flat on the floor. • Instruct the participant to forward flex at the hips and abdomen while rotating to one side (either left or right). • The participant should then perform a powerful throw of a 2.7-kg medicine ball to the contralateral side. • Repeat the throw on both the left and right sides for a full evaluation. • Repeat the test 3 times on each side and record the average distance of the three trials.
41.reference: Shinkle, J., Nesser, T. W., Demchak, T. J., & McMannus, D. M. (2012). Effect of core strength on the measure of power in the extremities. The Journal of Strength & Conditioning Research , 26(2), 373-380.

42.key_points: • The patient is placed prone with the arm abducted to 90 degrees and the elbow flexed to 90 degrees. • The dynamometer is placed 2cm proximal to the joint line of the wrist.
42.protocol_steps: • Position the patient prone on the bed with the arm abducted to 90 degrees and the elbow flexed to 90 degrees (prone 90:90 position). • Move the patient to one side of the bed so that their elbow is in line with the edge of the bed. • Place a rolled up towel under the distal humerus to ensure the humerus is parallel to the bed. • Ensure the patient's hips and torso remain stable throughout the test, avoiding any compensatory movements. • Place the dynamometer against the palmar side of the forearm, just proximal to the wrist. • The examiner should be in a half-kneeling position, stabilising the arm holding the dynamometer by bracing the elbow against the anterior superior iliac spine to securely fix the dynamometer in place. • Instruct the patient to push as hard as possible into the dynamometer, performing maximal internal rotation of the shoulder. The examiner may use their free arm to support the patients elbow, preventing them from adducting their arm. • Hold the internal rotation effort for 3 seconds, then allow the patient to rest for 20 seconds. • Repeat the test three times and record the average of the three maximal efforts.
42.reference: Cools, A. M., Vanderstukken, F., Vereecken, F., Duprez, M., Heyman, K., Goethals, N., & Johansson, F. (2016). Eccentric and isometric shoulder rotator cuff strength testing using a hand‐held dynamometer: reference values for overhead athletes. Knee Surgery, Sports Traumatology, Arthroscopy , 24 (12), 3838-3847.

43.key_points: The examiner is in a half kneeling position, stablising their testing arm by placing their elbow against the anterior, superior iliac spine of the hip. Ensure the shoulder is abducted to 90 degrees and the elbow is flexed to 90 degrees. The dynamometer is placed 2cm proximal to the ulnar styloid.
43.protocol_steps: • Position the patient prone on the bed with the arm abducted to 90 degrees and the elbow flexed to 90 degrees (prone 90:90 position). • Move the patient to one side of the bed so that their elbow is in line with the edge of the bed. • Place a rolled up towel under the distal humerus to ensure the humerus is parallel to the bed. • Ensure the patient's hips and torso remain stable throughout the test, avoiding any compensatory movements. • Place the dynamometer against the dorsal side of the forearm, just proximal to the ulnar styloid. • The examiner should be in a half-kneeling position, stabilising the arm holding the dynamometer by bracing the elbow against the anterior superior iliac spine to securely fix the dynamometer in place. • Instruct the patient to push as hard as possible into the dynamometer, performing maximal external rotation of the shoulder. The examiner may use their free arm to support the patients elbow, preventing them from adducting their arm. • Hold the internal rotation effort for 3 seconds, then allow the patient to rest for 20 seconds. • Repeat the test three times and record the average of the three maximal efforts.
43.reference: Cools, A. M., Vanderstukken, F., Vereecken, F., Duprez, M., Heyman, K., Goethals, N., & Johansson, F. (2016). Eccentric and isometric shoulder rotator cuff strength testing using a hand‐held dynamometer: reference values for overhead athletes. Knee Surgery, Sports Traumatology, Arthroscopy , 24 (12), 3838-3847.

44.key_points: The patient is in a prone position with the shoulder abducted to 90 degrees. The non-testing hand is positioned behind their back. Forehead supported on a yoga block or rolled-up towel.
44.protocol_steps: • Position the patient prone, with the shoulder abducted to 180 degrees (arm directly overhead), and the hand placed on the measurement device (e.g. dynamometer, force plate or weighing scales) - ensuring the device was zeroed prior to this step. • The non-testing hand should rest by the patient’s side, keeping the body stable. • Place a yoga block or towel under the patient’s forehead for stabilization. • Instruct the patient to push down as hard and as fast as possible with the testing arm. • Hold the push for 3 seconds, followed by a 20-second rest. • Repeat the test 3 times, and average the results.
44.reference: Morrison, G., Ashworth, B., & Taylor-Kaveney, T. (2021). The validity of the sphygmomanometer for shoulder strength assessment in amateur rugby union players. Physical Therapy in Sport , 47 , 59-65.

45.equipment: Handheld dynamometer. Alternatives: Force plate/bathroom scales.
45.key_points: The patient is in a prone position with the shoulder abducted to 135 degrees. The non-testing hand is positioned behind their back. Forehead supported on a yoga block or rolled-up towel.
45.protocol_steps: • Position the patient prone, with the shoulder abducted to 180 degrees (arm directly overhead), and the hand placed on the measurement device (e.g. dynamometer, force plate or weighing scales) - ensuring the device was zeroed prior to this step. • The non-testing hand should rest by the patient’s side, keeping the body stable. • Place a yoga block or towel under the patient’s forehead for stabilization. • Instruct the patient to push down as hard and as fast as possible with the testing arm. • Hold the push for 3 seconds, followed by a 20-second rest. • Repeat the test 3 times, and average the results.
45.reference: Morrison, G., Ashworth, B., & Taylor-Kaveney, T. (2021). The validity of the sphygmomanometer for shoulder strength assessment in amateur rugby union players. Physical Therapy in Sport , 47 , 59-65.

46.key_points: The patient is in a prone position with the shoulder abducted to 180 degrees. The non-testing hand is positioned by their side. Forehead supported on a yoga block or rolled-up towel.
46.protocol_steps: • Position the patient prone, with the shoulder abducted to 180 degrees (arm directly overhead), and the hand placed on the measurement device (e.g. dynamometer, force plate or weighing scales) - ensuring the device was zeroed prior to this step. • The non-testing hand should rest by the patient’s side, keeping the body stable. • Place a yoga block or towel under the patient’s forehead for stabilization. • Instruct the patient to push down as hard and as fast as possible with the testing arm. • Hold the push for 3 seconds, followed by a 20-second rest. • Repeat the test 3 times, and average the results.
46.reference: Morrison, G., Ashworth, B., & Taylor-Kaveney, T. (2021). The validity of the sphygmomanometer for shoulder strength assessment in amateur rugby union players. Physical Therapy in Sport , 47 , 59-65.

47.equipment: Phone inclinometer: We recommend Rate Fast Goniometer for iOS or Angle Meter for Android. Both are free to download.
47.key_points: The patient should be positioned supine on a treatment table with the shoulder abducted to 90 degrees and the elbow flexed to 90 degrees. The examiner prevents shoulder girdle protraction with their free hand and stabilizes the patient’s elbow against their thigh. The measurement is taken by placing the inclinometer on the dorsal (posterior) aspect of the forearm.
47.protocol_steps: • Position the patient lying supine on a treatment table, with their shoulder abducted to 90 degrees and elbow flexed to 90 degrees. • The examiner should use their free hand to prevent protraction of the shoulder girdle throughout the test. • Rest the patient’s elbow against the examiner’s thigh to ensure stability during the movement. • Instruct the patient to actively internally rotate their arm as far as they can until pressure is felt on the examiners hand as the patient begins to protract their shoulder.• Place the inclinometer on the posterior (dorsal) aspect of the forearm to measure the maximum internal rotation angle. • Record the internal rotation measurement.
47.reference: Werner, B. C., Holzgrefe, R. E., Griffin, J. W., Lyons, M. L., Cosgrove, C. T., Hart, J. M., & Brockmeier, S. F. (2014). Validation of an innovative method of shoulder range-of-motion measurement using a smartphone clinometer application. Journal of shoulder and elbow surgery, 23(11), e275-e282.

48.equipment: Phone inclinometer: We recommend Rate Fast Goniometer for iOS or Angle Meter for Android. Both are free to download. Dowel or broom handle.
48.key_points: • The patient should sit with their back firmly against a solid surface, legs out straight, and hips wedged back as far as possible. • Hands should be placed acromion-width apart on the broom handle or dowel, with palms facing down and elbows fully extended.
48.protocol_steps: • Position the patient seated on the floor with their back against a solid surface and legs extended straight in front of them. • Ensure the patient’s hips are wedged as far back as possible to maintain a stable posture. • Instruct the patient to hold a broom handle or dowel with both hands, ensuring their hands are acromion-width apart, palms facing down, and elbows locked out straight. • Ask the patient to raise their hands above their head, keeping the elbows fully extended throughout the movement. • Measure the maximum flexion angle by placing the phone inclinometer against the ulnar styloid, with the long edge of the phone aligned along the forearm. • Record the maximum flexion angle.

49.key_points: The patient lies prone on the floor with the testing arm abducted to 90 degrees and the elbow fully extended. The testing arm holds a weight equivalent to 2% of the patient’s body weight. The patient must keep their chest and hips on the floor throughout the test to prevent spinal rotation.
49.protocol_steps: • Position the patient prone on the floor with their feet shoulder-width apart. • The testing arm should be abducted to 90 degrees, with the elbow fully extended and holding a weight equivalent to 2% of their body weight. • Place the patient’s forehead and testing arm on yoga blocks for support. • Instruct the patient to place their non-testing arm behind their back. • Ask the patient to lift the weight off the block by 5 cm and hold that position for as long as possible. • Monitor the patient to ensure their chest and hips remain on the floor, avoiding any spinal rotation. • If the patient’s hand touches the block once, give a verbal warning. If the hand touches the block a second time, the test is stopped. • Record the duration of the hold time before the test is terminated.
49.reference: Evans, N. A., Dressler, E., & Uhl, T. (2018). An electromyography study of muscular endurance during the posterior shoulder endurance test. Journal of Electromyography and Kinesiology , 41 , 132-138.

50.key_points: The test is performed while seated with the back supported against the chair and feet flat on the ground. A 3 kg medicine ball is used, and the starting point of the tape measure is determined by where the patient drops the ball with their elbows fully extended in front of them. A 20-second rest period is allowed between each trial.
50.protocol_steps: • The subject sits on a chair with the backrest against a wall. The patient is tested in a sitting position with their back against the backrest and feet flat on the ground at all times. • The subject holds a 3kg medicine ball in both hands with arms fully extended away from their chest, and drops the ball - this denotes the 0 point for the tape measure. • Instruct the subject to start with the ball against their chest and then push the ball away from their chest as forcefully as possible, using a motion similar to a basketball chest pass. • Emphasize releasing the ball at the correct angle (45 degrees) for maximum distance. • The subject forcefully pushes the 3kg ball away from their chest, and the spot where the front end of the ball lands is recorded on the tape measure. • Allow a 20-second rest between each trial. • The subject performs 3 trials, with each throw measured. • Record the distances for all three throws and calculate the mean score for analysis.
50.reference: Harris, C., Wattles, A. P., DeBeliso, M., Sevene-Adams, P. G., Berning, J. M., & Adams, K. J. (2011). The seated medicine ball throw as a test of upper body power in older adults. The Journal of Strength & Conditioning Research , 25 (8), 2344-2348.

51.equipment: Phone inclinometer: We recommend Rate Fast Goniometer for iOS or Angle Meter for Android. Both are free to download.
51.key_points: This test assesses thoracic rotation in a lumbar-locked position to isolate thoracic movement The participant is positioned in a quadruped stance with the hips resting back onto the heels. Measurements are taken using an inclinometer or similar tool for both right and left thoracic rotation.
51.protocol_steps: Position the participant sitting back onto their heels with knees slightly apart and forearms parallel to the testing surface. Ensure elbows are placed just above the knees under the shoulders. • Assist the participant in achieving a neutral head position and instruct them to maintain this posture throughout the test. • For right thoracic rotation, place the inclinometer on the spinous process of T1 and set it to zero. • Instruct the participant to place their right hand behind their head and rotate their torso to the right as far as possible while maintaining the lumbar-locked position. • Hold this position while recording the measurement from the inclinometer. • Repeat the procedure for left thoracic rotation, with the participant placing their left hand behind their head and rotating to the left. • Ensure consistent encouragement and posture correction for accurate and reliable measurements. Prototcol adapted from: Feijen, S., Kuppens, K., Tate, A., Baert, I., Struyf, T., & Struyf, F. (2018). Intra-and interrater reliability of the ‘lumbar-locked thoracic rotation test’in competitive swimmers ages 10 through 18 years. Physical Therapy in Sport , 32 , 140-144.

52.equipment: Phone inclinometer: We recommend Rate Fast Goniometer for iOS or Angle Meter for Android. Both are free to download.
52.key_points: • The patient should be positioned supine on a treatment table with the shoulder abducted to 90 degrees and the elbow flexed to 90 degrees. • The measurement is taken by placing the inclinometer on the palmar (anterior) aspect of the forearm.
52.protocol_steps: • Position the patient lying supine on a treatment table. • Ensure the patient's shoulder is abducted to 90 degrees and the elbow is flexed to 90 degrees. • Instruct the patient to actively externally rotate their arm as far as they can while maintaining the starting position. • Place the inclinometer on the anterior (palmar) aspect of the forearm to measure the maximum external rotation angle. • Record the external rotation measurement.
52.reference: Werner, B. C., Holzgrefe, R. E., Griffin, J. W., Lyons, M. L., Cosgrove, C. T., Hart, J. M., & Brockmeier, S. F. (2014). Validation of an innovative method of shoulder range-of-motion measurement using a smartphone clinometer application. Journal of shoulder and elbow surgery , 23 (11), e275-e282.

53.key_points: The test is performed in the “Biering-Sorensen position” with the upper body extending out over the end of a test bench. The edge of the bed is aligned with the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) of the patient. The ankles are secured with a traction belt during the test. The arms are crossed over the chest with hands resting on the opposite shoulders.
53.protocol_steps: • Position the subject with the ASIS in line with the end of the bed, the ankles secured, and the arms supporting the upper body on a chair or box. • Instruct the subject to cross their arms over their chest, resting hands on the opposite shoulders. • The test ends when 1 - The upper body drops from the horizontal position, 2 - The participant requests to stop, 3 - The subject fails to maintain form after one verbal warning. • Provide verbal encouragement throughout the test to help the subject maintain high-intensity effort.
53.reference: McGill, S., Belore, M., Crosby, I., & Russell, C. (2010). Clinical tools to quantify torso flexion endurance: Normative data from student and firefighter populations. Occupational Ergonomics , 9 (1), 55-61.

54.key_points: The test is conducted with the subject prone, elbows directly under the shoulders, and hands together to form a “V” with the forearms. The subject must keep a straight and rigid torso throughout the test. The aim is to hold the position for as long as possible while maintaining proper form.
54.protocol_steps: Position the subject prone with elbows under the shoulders and hands together to form a “V” with the forearms. Instruct the subject to bridge off the ground, keeping the torso straight and rigid. The test ends when: 1 - The subject can no longer maintain a straight back and the hips begin to drop towards the floor, 2 - The participant requests to stop, 3 - The subject fails to maintain form after one verbal warning. Provide verbal encouragement throughout the test to help the subject sustain high-intensity effort.
54.reference: McGill, S., Belore, M., Crosby, I., & Russell, C. (2010). Clinical tools to quantify torso flexion endurance: Normative data from student and firefighter populations. Occupational Ergonomics , 9 (1), 55-61.

55.key_points: Legs are extended, with the top foot placed in front of the lower foot for support. The subject supports themselves on one elbow and feet while lifting their hips off the floor to form a straight line along the body length. The uninvolved arm is crossed over the chest with the hand resting on the opposite shoulder. The objective is to maintain a straight line from shoulders to feet.
55.protocol_steps: • Position the subject lying on their side with legs extended. The top foot should be placed in front of the lower foot for support. • Instruct the subject to support themselves on one elbow and feet, lifting their hips off the floor to create a straight line from head to feet. • Have the subject cross the uninvolved arm over their chest, with the hand placed on the opposite shoulder. • The test ends when: 1 - The subject loses the straight-back posture and the hip returns to the ground, 2 - The participant requests to stop, 3 - The subject fails to maintain form after one verbal warning. • Provide verbal encouragement throughout the test to help the subject maintain high-intensity effort.
55.reference: McGill, S., Belore, M., Crosby, I., & Russell, C. (2010). Clinical tools to quantify torso flexion endurance: Normative data from student and firefighter populations. Occupational Ergonomics , 9 (1), 55-61.

56.key_points: • Patient lies prone with forehead resting on their hands, elbows and forearms flat on the floor throughout. • The knee of the working leg is bent to 90 degrees so the tibia is vertical. • The ankle strap is positioned 2cm proximal to the medial malleolus. • The cable pulls the tibia into internal rotation — the foot falls outward to the end of the patient's available range before initiating the pull. • The patient externally rotates the hip, pulling the tibia inward, ensuring it passes beyond vertical — ideally to approximately 45 degrees so the foot is directly over or past the non-working knee. • The non-working leg remains flat on the floor throughout. • Controlled movement is essential — avoid hip rotation or trunk compensation.
56.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test and demonstrate the movement. Allow the patient a few practice repetitions to establish correct technique and range. • Select a load expected to result in failure in less than 10 repetitions. • Patient lies prone with forehead on hands, elbows and forearms flat on the floor. The working knee is bent to 90 degrees with the ankle strap attached 2cm proximal to the medial malleolus. • The patient lowers the tibia under control into full internal rotation — allowing the foot to fall outward to the end of their available range. • The patient then externally rotates the hip, pulling the tibia inward past vertical to approximately 45 degrees. • Return under control back to full internal rotation. • Repeat until volitional failure, defined as the inability to pull the tibia beyond vertical or maintain proper form. • Record the weight and the number of repetitions completed. • Allow sufficient rest before testing the contralateral leg using the same protocol.

57.key_points: • Adjust the machine so the knee pad sits just proximal to the ankle, and the pivot axis aligns with the knee joint. • The working leg should move through full comfortable extension. • The non-working leg remains relaxed and does not assist. Hold both handles. • Maintain an upright torso against the backrest, avoiding trunk lean or rotation. • Controlled movement is essential—avoid using momentum.
57.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test and demonstrate the movement. Allow the patient a submaximal practice repetition to ensure understanding and comfort. • Select a load expected to result in failure between 3–5 repetitions. The load used must include total system weight, defined as the weight on the machine plus the weight of any attachments. • If failure occurs beyond 20 repetitions, increase the load and repeat the test after adequate rest. • Patient sits with the back fully supported, knee aligned with the machine axis, and the working leg pad positioned just above the ankle. • The patient extends the knee fully against resistance, maintaining control and upright posture. • Lower the leg under control back to the starting position. • Repeat until volitional failure, defined as inability to complete a repetition with proper technique or full knee extension without compensatory trunk movement. • Record the total system weight and the number of repetitions completed. • Allow sufficient rest before testing the contralateral leg using the same protocol.

58.key_points: • Adjust the machine so the knee pad sits just proximal to the ankle and the pivot axis aligns with the knee joint. • The working leg moves from full extension to 90 degrees of flexion. • The non-working leg stays in full extension. • Maintain an upright torso against the backrest, avoiding trunk lean or rotation. • Controlled movement is essential—avoid jerking or swinging.
58.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test and demonstrate the movement.
