Allow the patient a submaximal practice repetition to ensure understanding and comfort. • Select a load expected to result in failure between 3–5 repetitions. The load used must include total system weight, defined as the weight on the machine. • If failure occurs beyond 20 repetitions, increase the load and repeat the test after adequate rest. • Patient sits with back fully supported, holding the handles, knee aligned with the machine axis, and the working leg pad positioned 2cm above the maleoli. • The non-working leg remains in full extension throughout the test. • The patient flexes the knee fully against resistance, until they reach a 90 degree angle. • Control back to the starting position. • Repeat until volitional failure, defined as the inability to complete a repetition with proper technique or 90 degrees flexion without compensatory trunk movement. • Record the total system weight and the number of repetitions completed. • Allow sufficient rest before testing the contralateral leg using the same protocol.

59.equipment: Barbell. Weight plates. Knee-height box or step for front foot support. Safety pins in a squat rack or power rack.
59.key_points: • Adjust the safety pins to just below the patient's shoulder height. • Emphasise full plantarflexion of the working leg; observe this maximum height before testing without the barbell to ensure consistency. • The front foot rests on a knee-height box for balance only—do not load weight through this foot. • The non-working foot remains stationary and assists only for balance. • Controlled movement is essential—avoid bouncing or using momentum.
59.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test and demonstrate the movement. Allow the patient to perform a few practice repetitions without weight to familiarise with range and ensure correct technique. • Set the barbell on safety pins just below the patient's shoulder height. • Select a load expected to result in failure between 3–5 repetitions. The load used must include total system weight, defined as the barbell plus all plates. • Patient positions the barbell in a high bar position with the bar just above the spine of scapula. The working foot should be flat on the floor and the front foot on the knee-height box or bench for balance. • The patient performs a calf raise by plantarflexing the ankle of the working leg as high as possible, reaching the previously observed maximum height. • Lower the heel under control back to the floor without letting the bar rest on the safety pins during the movement. • Repeat until volitional failure, defined as the inability to reach full plantarflexion or maintain proper form. • Record the total system weight and the number of repetitions completed. • Allow sufficient rest before testing the contralateral leg using the same protocol.

60.equipment: Dumbbell. Stopwatch. Yoga block or similar support.
60.key_points: • Patient lies supine on the floor with the shoulder abducted to 90 degrees and elbow bent to 90 degrees. The joint line of the wrist should rest on the yoga block. • Working arm holds the dumbbell; non-working hand rests on the stomach.
60.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test and demonstrate the position. Allow the patient to practice with a light weight to ensure correct posture and technique. • Start with a dumbbell equivalent to 5% of the patient's bodyweight. • Instruct the patient to lift the dumbbell 1-2cm off the yoga block for 10 seconds. • The objective is to determine the heaviest weight the patient can hold for 10 seconds with proper form, up to a target of 12.5% of bodyweight. • Record the maximum weight held successfully for 10 seconds. • Repeat the test on the contralateral arm using the same protocol.

61.key_points: • Stand with your back against a wall to maintain an upright posture. • Elbow of the working arm should be at shoulder height, bent to 90°, with forearm facing forward and palm pointing toward the floor. • The non-working hand rests on the stomach for support. • The test focuses on holding the external rotation position with proper form.
61.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test and demonstrate the position. Allow the patient to practice with a light weight to ensure correct posture and technique. • The patient's back should not be flush with the wall. The objective is to test in a scapula plane. • Start with a dumbbell equivalent to 5% of the patient's bodyweight. • Instruct the patient to hold the dumbbell in the correct position for 10 seconds while maintaining upright posture and proper shoulder alignment. • The objective is to determine the heaviest weight the patient can hold in this position for 10 seconds with correct form. • Record the maximum weight held successfully for 10 seconds. • Repeat the test on the contralateral arm using the same protocol.

62.key_points: • Stand with your back flat against a wall to maintain an upright posture. • Begin with the dumbbell in a hammer curl position — elbow fully extended, palm facing inward toward the body. • As you flex the elbow, rotate the forearm so that the wrist is fully supinated at the top — palm facing toward the face. • Controlled movement is essential — avoid shoulder elevation, trunk lean, or using momentum.
62.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test and demonstrate the movement. Allow the patient a few practice repetitions to ensure correct technique. • Select a dumbbell load expected to result in failure between 3–5 repetitions. • Patient stands with their back flat against the wall, holding the dumbbell in a hammer curl position with the elbow in full extension. • The patient flexes the elbow through its full range of motion, rotating the forearm to finish with the wrist fully supinated and the elbow in maximal flexion. • Lower the dumbbell under control, rotating back to the hammer curl position, until the elbow returns to full extension. • Repeat until volitional failure, defined as the inability to complete a full repetition with proper technique or maintain control throughout the movement. • Record the dumbbell weight and the number of repetitions completed. • Allow sufficient rest before testing the contralateral arm using the same protocol.

63.key_points: • Patient lies supine on a flat bench or the floor. • Working arm starts with shoulder flexed to 90° and elbow fully extended. • The non-working arm rests on the stomach. • Controlled movement is essential—avoid shoulder movement or trunk compensation. • Full elbow extension should be achieved at the top; lower until the dumbbell touches the floor or bench.
63.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test and demonstrate the movement. Allow the patient a few practice repetitions to ensure correct technique. • Select a dumbbell load expected to result in failure between 3–5 repetitions. The load used must include the weight of the dumbbell only. • Patient lies supine with shoulder flexed to 90° and elbow fully extended. • The patient lowers the dumbbell under control until the dumbbell touches the bench or floor, then extends the elbow back to full extension. • Repeat until volitional failure, defined as inability to complete a repetition with proper form or reaching full extension. • Record the dumbbell weight and the number of repetitions completed. • Allow sufficient rest before testing the contralateral arm using the same protocol.

67.key_points: • Patient stands upright with feet shoulder-width apart. • Barbell rests across the upper trapezius (high-bar position). • Emphasise controlled lumbar extension, avoiding excessive hip drive or knee flexion. • Maintain a neutral spine throughout; avoid hyperextension or rounding. • Full range is from upright stance to at least 45 degrees of torso inclination (or comfortable limit). • Highly recommended: the patient should have trained this movement previously and successfully achieved the target on the Sorensen hold before performing this test.
67.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test and demonstrate the movement. Allow the patient to perform an unloaded practice set using a broom handle or PVC pipe to establish correct technique and set the safety bars to this height. • Select a load expected to result in failure in less than 10 repetitions. The load used must include total system weight, defined as the barbell plus plates. • Patient stands upright with the barbell across the upper back. Feet are shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent. • The patient bends forward at the hips, keeping the spine neutral, lowering the torso to at least 45 degrees of inclination (or their comfortable maximum range). • Return to upright stance by extending through the lumbar spine and hips, maintaining control. • Repeat until volitional failure, defined as the inability to maintain neutral spine or complete full extension with proper form. • Record the total system weight and the number of repetitions completed.

68.key_points: • Patient stands with feet hip-width apart, barbell over mid-foot. • Any grip is acceptable. Chalk and/or straps are also acceptable. • Maintain a neutral spine throughout — avoid rounding of the lumbar or thoracic spine. • The bar should remain close to the shins and travel in a vertical path throughout the lift. • Hips and shoulders should rise at the same rate during the initial pull — avoid the hips shooting up early. • Full lockout is achieved when the hips and knees are fully extended and the shoulders are behind the bar. • Highly recommended: the patient should have trained this movement previously and successfully achieved the target on the Sorensen hold before performing this test.
68.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test and demonstrate the movement. Allow the patient to perform an unloaded practice set to establish correct technique. • Select a load expected to result in failure in less than 10 repetitions. The load used must include total system weight, defined as the barbell plus all plates. • Patient stands with feet hip-width apart, barbell over mid-foot. Hip hinge to grip the bar, setting the hips, bracing the core, and establishing a neutral spine before initiating the lift. • The patient drives through the floor, keeping the bar close to the body, extending the hips and knees simultaneously until full lockout is achieved. • Lower the bar under control back to the floor. The patient may not bounce the bar off the floor between repetitions. • Repeat until volitional failure, defined as the inability to maintain neutral spine, complete full lockout, or control the descent. • Record the total system weight and the number of repetitions completed.

69.key_points: • Patient stands with feet hip-width apart, holding the barbell so it rests against the mid-thigh with elbows fully extended. • Double overhand grip must be used. • Hinge at the hips until a stretch is felt in the hamstrings — typically at least 45 degrees, ideally with the back near parallel to the floor. Maintain this position throughout the set. • Maintain a neutral spine throughout — avoid rounding of the lumbar or thoracic spine. • Row until the elbows pass behind the torso, retracting the scapula at the top of the movement. • Controlled movement is essential — avoid using momentum or hip drive to initiate the row.
69.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test and demonstrate the movement. Allow the patient to perform a few practice repetitions with a light load to establish correct technique and torso position. • Select a load expected to result in failure in less than 10 repetitions. The load used must include total system weight, defined as the barbell plus all plates. • Patient stands with feet hip-width apart, holding the barbell against the mid-thigh with elbows fully extended using a double overhand grip. The patient hinges at the hips until a stretch is felt in the hamstrings. • The patient rows the barbell until the elbows pass behind the torso. • Lower the bar under control back to the starting position until the elbows are fully extended. • Repeat until volitional failure, defined as the inability to maintain torso position, neutral spine, or complete a full repetition with proper form. • Record the total system weight and the number of repetitions completed.

70.equipment: Dumbbell. Incline bench set to 30 degrees.
70.key_points: • Set the bench to 30 degrees. Patient lies prone with feet hip-width apart on the floor. • The chin must not rest on the bench at any point. • Non-working arm is placed behind the back throughout the set. • Start position is with the working arm in full extension — the dumbbell may be touching the floor. • Row until the dumbbell makes contact with the bench. • Controlled movement is essential — the dumbbell may not bounce off the floor between repetitions.
70.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test and demonstrate the movement. Allow the patient a few practice repetitions to ensure correct technique and range. • Select a dumbbell load expected to result in failure in less than 10 repetitions. • Patient lies prone on the bench set to 30 degrees, feet hip-width apart, chin clear of the bench, and non-working arm placed behind the back. • Starting with the working arm in full extension, the patient rows the dumbbell until it makes contact with the bench. • Lower the dumbbell under control back to full extension. The dumbbell may not bounce off the floor between repetitions. • A repetition does not count if the dumbbell does not make contact with the bench. • Repeat until volitional failure, defined as the inability to complete a full repetition with proper form, row the dumbbell to bench contact, or control the descent. • If the dumbbell fails to make contact with the bench for 2 consecutive repetitions, the test is terminated. • Record the dumbbell weight and the number of repetitions completed. • Allow sufficient rest before testing the contralateral arm using the same protocol.

71.equipment: Barbell. Weight plates. Flat bench with uprights or squat rack. Safety pins must be used.
71.key_points: • Any grip width is acceptable providing the thumbs are wrapped around the bar. • Feet must be flat on the floor throughout the lift. • The back may be arched but both glutes must remain in contact with the bench at all times. • The bar must be lowered to touch the chest — a repetition does not count if the bar does not make contact with the chest. • The bar must be pressed to full lockout. • Controlled movement is essential — the bar may not bounce off the chest.
71.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test and demonstrate the movement. Allow the patient to perform a warm up set with a light load to establish correct technique and set the safety pins to the correct height. • Select a load expected to result in failure in less than 10 repetitions. The load used must include total system weight, defined as the barbell plus all plates. • Patient lies supine on the bench with feet flat on the floor. The bar is unracked with elbows fully extended. • The patient lowers the bar under control to touch the chest. • The patient presses the bar to full lockout. • The bar may not bounce off the chest between repetitions. • A repetition does not count if the bar does not make contact with the chest or full lockout is not achieved. • Repeat until volitional failure, defined as the inability to complete a full repetition with proper form, touch the chest, or achieve full lockout. • Record the total system weight and the number of repetitions completed.

72.key_points: • Patient lies supine on the floor with one knee bent so the heel of the bent leg is in line with the joint line of the opposite knee. The other leg is then brought in to meet it. • The non-working arm rests across the chest throughout the set. • Start position is with the working arm in full extension. • Lower the dumbbell under control until the elbow touches the floor. • Return to full lockout — a repetition does not count if full extension is not achieved. • Controlled movement is essential — avoid trunk rotation or shoulder compensation.
72.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test and demonstrate the movement. Allow the patient a few practice repetitions to ensure correct technique and positioning. • Select a dumbbell load expected to result in failure in less than 10 repetitions. • Patient lies supine on the floor in the correct position with the non-working arm across the chest and the working arm in full extension. • The patient lowers the dumbbell under control until the elbow touches the floor. • The patient returns to full lockout. • A repetition does not count if the elbow does not touch the floor or full lockout is not achieved. • Repeat until volitional failure, defined as the inability to complete a full repetition with proper form, touch the floor with the elbow, or achieve full lockout. • Record the dumbbell weight and the number of repetitions completed. • Allow sufficient rest before testing the contralateral arm using the same protocol.

73.equipment: Barbell. Weight plates. Squat rack. Safety pins must be used.
73.key_points: • Barbell is positioned in a high-bar position, resting along the spine of the scapula. • Any foot width or toe angle is acceptable. • The patient must reach depth — defined as the hip crease passing below the top of the knee. • The back may be inclined but must remain rigid throughout — avoid rounding of the lumbar or thoracic spine. • The bar must be returned to full lockout at the top — knees and hips fully extended. • Controlled movement is essential — avoid bouncing out of the bottom position.
73.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test and demonstrate the movement. Allow the patient to perform a warm up set with a light load to establish correct technique and set the safety pins to the correct height. • Select a load expected to result in failure in less than 10 repetitions. The load used must include total system weight, defined as the barbell plus all plates. • Patient unracks the bar and positions feet at their preferred stance width with toes angled as preferred. • The patient descends under control until the hip crease passes below the top of the knee. • The patient drives back to full lockout, extending the hips and knees simultaneously. • A repetition does not count if the hip crease does not pass below the top of the knee or full lockout is not achieved. • Repeat until volitional failure, defined as the inability to reach depth, achieve full lockout, or maintain proper form. • Record the total system weight and the number of repetitions completed.

74.key_points: • Test must be performed on a sled leg press. • The non-working leg remains off the sled and relaxed throughout. • Foot position should be mid-height on the platform, hip-width apart from the midline. • The patient must reach full depth — defined as 90 degrees of knee flexion. • The sled must be returned to full knee extension — avoid locking out the knee aggressively. • Controlled movement is essential — avoid bouncing at the bottom of the movement. • Total system weight is defined as the weight of the sled plus all plates.
74.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test and demonstrate the movement. Allow the patient to perform practice repetitions with an empty sled to establish correct technique, foot position, and set the safety guards to the correct depth. • Select a load expected to result in failure in less than 10 repetitions. The load used must include total system weight, defined as the sled plus all plates. • Patient sits in the leg press with the working foot positioned mid-height on the platform. The non-working leg is off the sled and relaxed. • The patient lowers the sled under control until 90 degrees of knee flexion is achieved. • The patient drives the sled back to full knee extension. • A repetition does not count if 90 degrees of knee flexion is not achieved or full extension is not reached. • Repeat until volitional failure, defined as the inability to reach 90 degrees, achieve full extension, or maintain proper form. • Record the total system weight and the number of repetitions completed. • Allow sufficient rest before testing the contralateral leg using the same protocol.

75.equipment: Pull up bar. Dip belt and weight plates (if required).
75.key_points: • Overhand (pronated) grip must be used, hands shoulder-width apart. • Each repetition must begin from a full dead hang with arms completely straight. • The chin must clearly pass above the bar at the top of each repetition. • The arms must return to full extension at the bottom of each repetition. • No kipping, swinging, or use of momentum is permitted. • No bending at the waist or knees throughout the set. • Resting in the dead hang position is permitted — resting the chin on the bar is not.
75.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test and demonstrate the movement. Allow the patient a few practice repetitions to establish correct technique. • If the patient can complete more than 10 bodyweight pull ups, additional load must be added using a dip belt and weight plates. • Total system weight is defined as the patient's bodyweight plus any additional load. • Patient grips the bar with an overhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart, and begins from a full dead hang. • The patient pulls until the chin clearly passes above the bar. • The patient lowers under control until the arms are fully extended before beginning the next repetition. • A repetition does not count if the chin does not clearly pass above the bar or the arms do not return to full extension. • Repeat until volitional failure, defined as the inability to bring the chin above the bar or return to a full dead hang with proper form. • Record the total system weight and the number of repetitions completed.

76.equipment: Lat pulldown machine. Single arm attachment.
76.key_points: • Overhand (pronated) grip must be used. • Patient sits with thighs secured under the pad, feet flat on the floor. • The non-working arm rests on the thigh throughout the set. • The torso must remain upright throughout — leaning backwards to generate momentum is not permitted. • Each repetition begins with the working arm in full extension. • Pull until the elbow is in line with the torso, retracting the scapula at the end of the movement. • Return to full arm extension under control before beginning the next repetition.
76.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test and demonstrate the movement. Allow the patient a few practice repetitions to establish correct technique and seat position. • Select a load expected to result in failure in less than 10 repetitions. • Patient sits with thighs secured, torso upright, non-working arm resting on the thigh, and working arm in full extension gripping the single arm attachment with an overhand grip. • The patient pulls until the elbow is in line with the torso, retracting the scapula at the end of the movement. • The patient returns under control to full arm extension before beginning the next repetition. • A repetition does not count if the elbow does not reach the line of the torso, full arm extension is not achieved, or the torso leans back to generate momentum. • Repeat until volitional failure, defined as the inability to complete a full repetition with proper form or maintain an upright torso. • Record the weight and the number of repetitions completed. • Allow sufficient rest before testing the contralateral arm using the same protocol.

77.equipment: Barbell. Weight plates. Squat rack. Safety pins must be used.
77.key_points: • Patient sits upright with back fully supported against the bench, feet flat on the floor. • Safety pins must be set to just below shoulder height. Use the warm up set to establish the correct pin height. • The barbell starts at shoulder height, unracked with elbows at approximately 90 degrees. • Press directly overhead to full elbow extension. • Lower the bar under control until it reaches just below chin height — this is the bottom of each repetition. • Avoid excessive lumbar extension or trunk lean to assist the press. • Controlled movement is essential.
77.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test and demonstrate the movement. Allow the patient a warm up set with a light load to establish correct technique and set the safety pins to just below shoulder height. • Select a load expected to result in failure in less than 10 repetitions. The load used must include total system weight, defined as the barbell plus all plates. • Patient sits upright with back fully supported, unracking the barbell at shoulder height with elbows at approximately 90 degrees. • The patient presses the bar directly overhead to full elbow extension. • The patient lowers the bar under control until it reaches just below chin height. • A repetition does not count if full elbow extension is not achieved or the bar does not descend to just below chin height. • Repeat until volitional failure, defined as the inability to reach full elbow extension or lower the bar to just below chin height with proper form. • Record the total system weight and the number of repetitions completed.

78.key_points: • Patient adopts a half-kneeling position, with the working side knee down. • In the start position, the torso must be vertical, the shin of the front leg vertical, and the thigh of the front leg parallel to the floor. • The end of the barbell rests on the shoulder of the working arm at the start position. • Press the bar away from the body until the arm reaches full extension. • Return the bar under control back to the shoulder. • The non-working arm may be used for balance only — it must not touch the bar, the floor, or the body. • Controlled movement is essential — avoid trunk lean or rotation to assist the press. • Record the weight of the plates only — do not include the weight of the bar.
78.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test and demonstrate the movement. Allow the patient a few practice repetitions with no load to establish correct technique and position. • Select a plate load expected to result in failure in less than 10 repetitions. • Patient adopts a half-kneeling position with the working side knee down, torso vertical, shin of the front leg vertical, and thigh of the front leg parallel to the floor. The barbell end rests on the shoulder of the working arm. • The patient presses the bar away from the body until the arm reaches full extension. • The patient returns the bar under control back to the shoulder. • A repetition does not count if full extension is not achieved or the non-working arm makes contact with the bar, floor, or body. • Repeat until volitional failure, defined as the inability to reach full extension or maintain proper position and form. • Record the plate weight and the number of repetitions completed. • Allow sufficient rest before testing the contralateral arm using the same protocol.

79.equipment: Barbell. Weight plates. Flat bench. Barbell pad, foam pad, mat or towel (permitted to reduce discomfort).
79.key_points: • Patient sits on the floor with legs straight and back resting against the bench. • Roll the barbell toward the patient so it sits directly above the hip crease. • Bend one knee so the heel of the bent leg is in line with the joint line of the other leg, then bring the other leg in to meet it. • Place elbows on the bench and lift the hips off the floor. • Lift the non-working leg off the floor, keeping it elevated throughout the set. • The top position is reached when the body is parallel to the floor — hips fully extended. • Lower the hips under control until the plates make contact with the floor between each repetition. • Controlled movement is essential — avoid trunk rotation or lateral hip drop.
79.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test and demonstrate the movement. Allow the patient a few practice repetitions to establish correct technique and foot position. • A barbell pad, foam pad, mat or towel may be placed between the barbell and the hip crease to reduce discomfort. • Select a load expected to result in failure in less than 10 repetitions. The load used must include total system weight, defined as the barbell plus all plates. • Patient sits on the floor with back against the bench, barbell positioned over the hip crease, and performs the setup as described above. • The patient drives the hips upward to the top position with the body parallel to the floor. • The patient lowers the hips under control until the plates make contact with the floor. • A repetition does not count if the hips do not reach parallel or the plates do not make contact with the floor. • Repeat until volitional failure, defined as the inability to reach the top position, maintain control, or complete a full repetition with proper form. • Record the total system weight and the number of repetitions completed. • Allow sufficient rest before testing the contralateral leg using the same protocol.

80.key_points: • Patient lies supine with hands across the chest throughout the set. • The ankle strap is positioned 2cm proximal to the malleolus with the clip attached on the posterior aspect, just above the calcaneus. • The cable must be set to the lowest height setting. • Both legs start in full extension with toes pointing upward. • The working leg pulls the knee toward the chest until the femur reaches vertical and the tibia is parallel with the floor — hip and knee at 90 degrees flexion. • The non-working leg must remain in full extension with toes pointing upward throughout — it cannot bend to push the foot into the floor. • Controlled movement is essential — avoid trunk flexion or lifting the head.
80.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test and demonstrate the movement. Allow the patient a few practice repetitions to establish correct technique and range. • Select a load expected to result in failure in less than 10 repetitions. • Patient lies supine with hands across the chest, ankle strap attached 2cm proximal to the malleolus with the clip on the posterior aspect, cable on the lowest setting, both legs in full extension with toes pointing upward. • The patient pulls the working knee toward the chest until the femur reaches vertical and the tibia is parallel with the floor. • The patient lowers the leg under control back to full extension with toes pointing upward. • A repetition does not count if the femur does not reach vertical, the tibia is not parallel with the floor, or the non-working leg bends at any point. • Repeat until volitional failure, defined as the inability to reach the required range or maintain proper form. • Record the weight and the number of repetitions completed. • Allow sufficient rest before testing the contralateral leg using the same protocol.

81.key_points: • Patient stands side-on to the cable machine with the working leg closest to it. • The cable must be set to the lowest height setting. • The ipsilateral arm holds the cable machine with the elbow fully extended and shoulder abducted to 90 degrees for balance. • The ankle strap is attached 2cm superior to the malleolus. • Both legs remain in full extension throughout. • The working leg starts at approximately 45 degrees of abduction and moves into adduction until it is directly in front of the standing leg. • The working leg does not touch the floor at any point during the set. • The torso must remain vertical throughout — avoid lateral trunk lean. • Controlled movement is essential — avoid using momentum or hip hitching.
81.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test and demonstrate the movement. Allow the patient a few practice repetitions to establish correct technique and balance. • Select a load expected to result in failure in less than 10 repetitions. • Patient stands side-on to the cable machine, cable on the lowest setting, ipsilateral arm holding the machine with elbow extended and shoulder abducted to 90 degrees, ankle strap attached 2cm superior to the malleolus. • Starting with the working leg at approximately 45 degrees of abduction, the patient adducts the leg until it is directly in front of the standing leg. • The patient returns the working leg under control back to approximately 45 degrees of abduction without touching the floor. • A repetition does not count if the working leg touches the floor, the knees bend, or the torso deviates from vertical. • Repeat until volitional failure, defined as the inability to reach the required range or maintain proper form. • Record the weight and the number of repetitions completed. • Allow sufficient rest before testing the contralateral leg using the same protocol.

82.key_points: • Patient stands side-on to the cable machine with the working leg furthest from it. • The cable must be set to the lowest height setting. • The contralateral arm holds the cable machine with the elbow fully extended and shoulder abducted to 90 degrees for balance. • The ankle strap is attached 2cm superior to the malleolus. • Both legs remain in full extension throughout. • The working leg starts directly in front of the standing leg and moves into abduction until it reaches approximately 45 degrees. • The working leg does not touch the floor at any point during the set. • The torso must remain vertical throughout — avoid lateral trunk lean. • Controlled movement is essential — avoid using momentum or hip hitching.
82.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test and demonstrate the movement. Allow the patient a few practice repetitions to establish correct technique and balance. • Select a load expected to result in failure in less than 10 repetitions. • Patient stands side-on to the cable machine, cable on the lowest setting, contralateral arm holding the machine with elbow extended and shoulder abducted to 90 degrees, ankle strap attached 2cm superior to the malleolus. • Starting with the working leg directly in front of the standing leg, the patient abducts the leg out to approximately 45 degrees. • The patient returns the working leg under control back to directly in front of the standing leg without touching the floor. • A repetition does not count if the working leg touches the floor, the knees bend, or the torso deviates from vertical. • Repeat until volitional failure, defined as the inability to reach the required range or maintain proper form. • Record the weight and the number of repetitions completed. • Allow sufficient rest before testing the contralateral leg using the same protocol.

83.key_points: • Patient lies prone with forehead resting on their hands, elbows and forearms flat on the floor throughout. • The knee of the working leg is bent to 90 degrees so the tibia is vertical. • The ankle strap is positioned 2cm proximal to the medial malleolus. • The cable pulls the tibia into external rotation — the foot falls inward to the end of the patient's available range before initiating the pull. • The patient internally rotates the hip, pushing the foot outward until the tibia passes beyond vertical into end range internal rotation. • The foot must pass beyond the non-working knee — the tibia must go beyond vertical. • The non-working leg remains flat on the floor throughout. • Controlled movement is essential — avoid hip rotation or trunk compensation.
83.protocol_steps: • Verbally explain the test and demonstrate the movement. Allow the patient a few practice repetitions to establish correct technique and range. • Select a load expected to result in failure in less than 10 repetitions. • Patient lies prone with forehead on hands, elbows and forearms flat on the floor. The working knee is bent to 90 degrees with the ankle strap attached 2cm proximal to the medial malleolus. • The patient lowers the tibia under control into full external rotation — allowing the foot to fall inward to the end of their available range. • The patient then internally rotates the hip, pushing the foot outward until the tibia passes beyond vertical into end range internal rotation. • A repetition does not count if the tibia does not pass beyond vertical. • Return under control back to full external rotation. • Repeat until volitional failure, defined as the inability to push the tibia beyond vertical or maintain proper form. • Record the weight and the number of repetitions completed. • Allow sufficient rest before testing the contralateral leg using the same protocol.
